# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. I want you to rec your weirdest movies please!

**Score:** 48
**Body:** I love weird movies. Some favorites are Tusk, Men, Old Boy, Eraserhead, Jacob’s Ladder, Fifth Element, Barbarian, The Perfect Host, Melancholia, Antiviral, Midsommar, The Cell, Beau is Afraid

Darker movies, darker comedies are fun. I don’t love gore unless it’s really exaggerated like a huge blood gush scene in Nightmare on Elm St.

Movies that most people consider to be weird I didn’t really love: Mulholland Drive (gasp, I know—I felt like it was made too much for the male gaze & it took me out of the story), everything everywhere all at once, Vivarium, Coherence, Hereditary (it felt basic, sorry!)

Thank you for your help

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Blue Velvet
  - maybe also the film ‘River’s Edge’ with an extremely strange Dennis Hopper.
2. Being John Malkovich (1999)

Adaptation (2002)

The Lobster (2015)

Phantom Thread (2017); not necessarily dark, but a little twisted depending on who you ask
3. Naked Lunch. Cronenberg's fever dream. Also check out Taxidermia, it's Hungarian body horror that'll mess with your head for days. Exaggerated gore, dark comedy, the whole nine yards.
4. Liquid Sky

Performance
5. Rubber. The plot is that a tire off the side off a highway somehow becomes alive and has the ability, and this is not a joke, to telepathically blow things/people up with it's mind and then goes on a murderous rampage. It is ridiculous and absolutely amazing, I am not saying that ironically.  
Another worth watching, if you like horror type stuff, the ABCs of death and holidays are great ones. They are both movies that are a compilation of horror themed short stories, one about every letter in the alphabet and the other being about all the holidays.
6. Mirrormask (2005)
7. The Cruise- 1998

Crumb

Both are documentaries
8. Mad God

Iron lung

House 1977

The lighthouse

Everything everywhere all at once

In the mouth of madness

Big trouble little china

From beyond

Prince of darkness 

Annihilation 

Color out of space

Dagon

Donnie darko

Glorious

Butt boy

Swiss army man
9. 12 Monkeys

Vivarium

## 2. I just watched How to make millions before grandma dies, and I pity those who haven’t.

**Score:** 28
**Body:** This is a thai movie, I underestimated this movie before watching it and I was completely blown away with how good this movie actually is. Great acting, excellent storytelling and hats-off to the director, this movie is peak and I suggest you to watch it.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. imma watch it later today.  thanks.
2. I rlly enjoyed how heartwarming n just beautifully emotional that movie was. Very easy to connect to characters too :))
  - Yes the movie really played with my emotions, the way she keeps her promise even though he doesn’t remember it, definitely a great movie.
3.  There is an amazing amount of really good Thai cinema that gets overlooked. I hope more start showing up on the streaming services.
4. I watched it theatres when it came out and I ugly cried so hard, snot everywhere. My boyfriend, that never ever cries in movies, even shed some tears. Not a dry eye in the theatre too.
  - I can totally imagine the scene in the theatre, I couldn’t contain my tears even at home. Such a beautiful movie.
5. i'm frugal but that title feels cruel, seriously
6. "sentimental coming of age drama" idk if I have the juice for that rn
7. that movie hit different, had me ugly crying in my living room at 2am 😭💀
  - That’s exactly my state right now, man what a movie.

## 3. Movies suggestions for my wife who grew up sheltered.

**Score:** 18
**Body:** My wife was raised apostolic Pentecostal and was not allowed to watch most movies or tv shows growing up (she’s never seen SpongeBob 😳). Her and I are planning to make movie lists for each other to have for date nights. Any suggestions of the best movies to show her? I have a list below of some movies Iv always liked, but would like some good suggestions. 

A little about her, she does not like anything horror (which is my favorite 😭) she also does not like anything that would be gory. Inglourious Basterds is definitely toeing the line of gore for her, but hopefully I can get away with it. She loves Lord of the Rings and Harry Potter. 

\- The Sandlot

\- The Hot Chick

\- Bridge to Terabithia

\-  A League of Their Own

\- Selena

\- Inglorious Bastards 

\- The Truman Show

\- Juno

\- Shawshank Redemption 

\- Get Out

\- Gladiator

\- Walk the Line

\- Night at the Museum

\- Jumanji

\- Castaway

\- The Dallas Buyers Club

\- Mrs. Doubtfire

\- The Birdcage

\- Too Wong Foo Thanks for Everything

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. List of movies I would recommend to anyone: 

Princess Bride  
  
Moulin Rouge  
  
Howl’s Moving Castle   
  
Your Name.  
  
Scott Pilgrim vs the World  
  
The Muppet Movie  
  
Ferris Bueller’s Day Off  
  
The original Star Wars trilogy  
  
Raiders of the Lost Ark  
  
Ghostbusters
  - 1000000% with Moulin Rouge ❤️❤️
2. The Princess Bride 
3. Big ( comedy classic)

Under the Tuscan Sun

100 Foot Journey (GORGEOUS drama that is surprisingly funny and you can practically smell the food off the screen)

Across the Universe ( Beatles movie where the characters are all from songs and some of it is sung ( the covers in some cases are almost better than the original). Absolutely stunning movie. 

Lion the Witch and the Wardrobe ( based on the Cs Lewis novel. Stunning, emotional and the soundtrack is really beautiful

Shawshank Redemption ( must see at least once.  On most people’s top ten of all time movies)

Princess Bride ( classic… hysterically funny but really sweet)

Field of Dreams ( classic drama that is incredibly inspirational with some really funny parts.
4. Ferris Bueller's Day Off (1986)

Spirited Away (2001)
5. JURASSIC PARK!
6. The Commitments
7. Throwing out there…
- Napoleon Dynamite 
- She’s the Man 
- Mean Girls
- Legally Blonde
- Clueless
- Mars Attacks 
- The Incredibles 
- Grease
8. Stand by Me
9. Heathers

Legend of Billie Jean

## 4. Modern westerns like No Country for Old Men and Wind River

**Score:** 18
**Body:** Me and my wife recently rewatched no country for old men and wind river. We absolutely love these movies. To me they give of modern western vibes to me. Could anyone suggest some similar movies with similar vibes to these movies?

Thanks everyone!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Hell or High Water if you haven’t watched it is exactly this.
2. Sicario is a good example. 

Pretty much anything with Josh Brolin at this point. 
3. Hell or High Water.
4. Hell or High Water is a great one. 
5. I’m on the Hell or High Water train too
6. The Last Stop in Yuma County (2023); but Hell or High Water should be your first watch as others have suggested
7. It could be argued that One Battle After Another fits this bill.

Eddington, too.
8. 'The Three Burials of Melquiades Estrada' (2005)

'Bring Me the Head of Afredo Garcia (1974)
9. Brokeback mountain
10. Does a mini series on Netflix count? If yes, try The English.

## 5. Movies similar to Adventures in Babysitting

**Score:** 16
**Body:** I’m looking for movies with a similar vibe to Adventures in Babysitting. I really enjoyed watching it so now I want to watch similar movies. Something light, chaotic and fun. Looking forward to your recommendations.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I think the best recommendation that has a similar vibe is ... Don't Tell Mom The Babysitter's Dead. Similar in that the kids end up on their own with an older sibling taking charge of increasingly crazy situations, with a race at the end to cover everything up and make everything look completely normal before the parents come home. It's a deadset classic, and the most comparable movie to Adventures in Babysitting. 😃
  - And Keith coogan is in both films
    - YES! Good point! I didn't even think to mention that. Even though, as I was replying, I was thinking "Dishes are done, man". 😂
2. I think you would love "Don't Tell Mom the Babysitter's Dead" - has that same chaotic energy with kids getting in crazy situations
3. Back to the Future 

Honey I shrunk the kids

Inner space

Harry and the Hendersons
4. Uncle Buck
5. The goonies
6. Planes, Trains and Automobiles
7. Can’t Buy Me Love 

License to Drive
8. Madhouse,
License to Drive,
Ferris Bueller’s Day Off

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Why is this guy in almost every British movie?

**Score:** 1156

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Because he's been a reliable screen presence for decades, his face is perfect for the types of roles he's usually in, and he commits. He gives the same commitment to Mad-Eye Moody as he would give Shakespeare. *Braveheart, 28 Days Later, Gangs of New York, In Bruges, The Banshees of Inisherin*, or his voice work in the criminally underlooked *Song of the Sea*, he's fantastic.
  - He is underappreciated for Lake Placid too. He was hilarious and his banter with Oliver Platt is awesome, they're both so very talented 
2. Because he's an excellent actor?
  - 28 Days Later and Gangs of New York. Two strong back to back performances.
    - In Bruges, Banshees, Harry Potter, so many.
3. This is an ultra rare picture. Gleeson hasn’t smiled on screen in years! Lol
4. Because the man’s a genius

His sons not bad either
5. A ton of Irish actors tend to work in the UK films industry by virtue of the fact the UK is considerably larger than Ireland and Irish people have free movement with the country.

Also Gleeson is a goated actor who any production would kill to have in their cast.
6. The Guard 😀
7. Because he's awesome

![gif](giphy|VK0dgpGgiddcI)

## 2. What are the best movies in the "old, retired ex-special forces guy fucks everyone up" genre?

**Score:** 1012

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Commando. The early classic.

"Major. If you want to see your daughter again you do what we say, right?"

"Wrong." *headshot*
  - This scene in the opening credits alone. 2026 could never.

https://i.redd.it/3yk181kouvig1.gif
  - “Don’t wake my friend, he’s DEAD tired”
Or
“You said you would kill me last!!”

“I lied”.
2. Taken
  - One of the best. Nobody involved in making it expected it to be any sort of hit 
3. “Nobody” was fun.
4. John Wick.  He may not be technically Special Forces, but it's the same genre.

Rambo (though I only saw the first and fourth ones)
5. The Equalizer 
6. RED was great
7. The Man from Nowhere (2010)

## 3. I found The Hateful Eight to be really entertaining, with Samuel L. Jackson delivering a great monologue and performance in it. What’s your view on this movie?

**Score:** 253

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Its a nice throwback to his 'Reservoir Dogs' days. Limited locations, simple story, big difference from the Kill Bill levels of productions that he's been doing. Also, gets better on repeat viewing 
  - This is also why I really like it. I like well written "interesting people in a room with so far unknown connections" stories.
    - Have you seen “Four Rooms?” It takes place in a hotel telling the story of 4 separate rooms all connected by the bellhop played by Tim Roth. Each room is directed by a different director. Quentin Tarantino,  Allison Anders, Alexandre Rockwell, and Robert Rodriguez.  It has an incredible cast as well.  Definitely give it a view.
    - Yeah, this and Reservoir Dogs could be stage plays.
    - If you watch it a second time knowing the plot, there’s a whole second film happening right behind the first.  You can pick up so much of the gang’s coordination in effort to get info out without blowing cover.  Little laughs or nods.  Subtle but it’s there.
2. I was absolutely captivated by it. Less so at the beginning and the end, but as the characters got more and more revealed I felt like i was watching true evil. SLJ in particular - absolutely morbid. Closer to horror vibes than anything else.
3. I'd love to see this adapted to the stage.
4. Went to the 70mm roadshow at Tempe Marketplace, and it was cold (for Arizona) and my wife and I were both sick. That movie was like chicken soup for the sordid soul. Loved it. And I love that it’s been on Netflix for years cus I’ve been able to watch it over and over. One of my favorites of his.
5. It’s in my top 3 Tarantino movies. Love the dialogue, the characters, the limited setting. Even loved Tarantino narrating.

Feels more like watching a play than a movie
6. I guess it was ok but not something I have much interest watching again.

## 4. This Is the End was a really entertaining flick, and Jonah Hill’s possession scene always cracks me up. What’s your take on this movie?

**Score:** 245
**Body:** And it also had several great cameos.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Absolutely love it.

Don’t think we will ever see another film like it, with that many actors choosing to play horrible parody versions of themselves

Such a fun movie and always a laugh riot.

“There have been some fatalities……” will never not make me lmao
  - "Jonah, you're fucking sucking balls, you're an Academy Award nominated person, you need to be selling that shit. Fatalities, give us some fatalities." 

"Fatalities."

"Okay, that was good."
2. Something...not that chill happened last night.
3. “Sick reference bro everyone knows your references are out of control” is a quote I use almost daily
4. Dear Guys, it's me, Jonah Hill, from Moneyball.
5. Michael Cera knows how to party
  - "Does this cocaine smell funny to you?"  *Blows a kilo into McLovin's face*
6. It's Hilarious! Several cameos? Except for the main cast,, almost everyone is a cameo.
7. James Franco didn’t suck any dick last night, now I know your lying
8. Worth a rewatch every year or two

## 5. “Wuthering Heights” is Hot and Disgusting

**Score:** 161

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The most impressive aspect of the novel for me is the manner in which Bronte structures it to frame the narrative. Nelly is telling the story to Lockwood who is recording it in his journal. 

This setup allows Bronte to craft an incredible experience for us readers who get much of story essentially third-hand from two narrators who we increasingly trust less as their own motives are revealed. 

Bronte wasn’t the first to manipulate the timeline of a story for narrative reasons: Scheherazade kept her head (literally) every night by doing so. 

But Bronte did it so masterfully that those 400 pages have served as a blueprint for how to tell a story since Wuthering Heights was published almost 180 years ago. Even disregarding its historical context, it is one of the greatest literary flexes of all time. 

If even the shadow of the wind disturbed by a passing iota of Bronte’s genius would be successfully conveyed by this film, then it would be worth watching. 

A Wuthering Heights film done properly might show up in the same dinner conversations as Casablanca or The Godfather. 

However- and feel free to call me jaded or snobbish (I am)- I envision this film spending most of its meals at your aunt Vivian’s condo with a box of wine and a slurred scene-by-scene breakdown of how much more or less hot it is compared to Fifty Fucking Shades of Grey.
  - I don’t give a fuck about this film but you’ve sold me on reading the book - thank you!
    - You absolutely won’t regret it!
      - I love the book, but I'd say it's not everyone's thing. Especially if they go into it expecting a sweeping escapist romance. 

It has romance in it, but it's dark and twisted. I definitely think everyone should try reading it, though.
        - Yeah, I felt the overall effect was frustration at the deplorable nature of the characters and the seeming inability of anyone to move on or have any life outside an artificially small circle of people and events.
  - You, sir, have a way with words. I will continue to not watch this film but it wasn’t all bad because it spawned this fantastic Reddit review.
2. No nudity? Count me out
3. "Yes, Emerald Fennell’s film adaptation of *Wuthering Heights* does actually open with the sight of a hanging man’s erect penis carving an all-too-distinct outline in his trousers... 

Historically – at least, in my opinion – Fennell’s largely good work has been tainted by empty provocation, from Carey Mulligan getting smothered to death in rape revenge thriller *Promising Young Woman* to Barry Keoghan’s grave shagging in *Saltburn*. I have enjoyed her movies, but left them feeling frustrated by the spectre of an itchy finger tapping away on an “outrage” button when it really didn’t need to."

Agree with GQ's movie critic, Ben Allen? 

Read more: [https://www.gq-magazine.co.uk/article/wuthering-heights-is-hot-and-disgusting](https://www.gq-magazine.co.uk/article/wuthering-heights-is-hot-and-disgusting)
  - I don’t know if I will be able to agree with GQ’s movie critic, Ben Allen, because I got burned out hearing about this movie a month ago and it’s only felt more and more cringe ever since. 
4. But will this version have conversations using only semaphore flag signals?

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. High and Low (1963) review

**Score:** 38
**Body:** Hello, this is my review for High and Low which is the best movie I have seen recently. I am looking for feedback on the writing because English is not my main language. Let me know what you think.

//

*High and Low* review:

For me, this movie is as close to a masterpiece as it can get.

What I loved is how it  surprises us with its structure. The first part of the movie is in closed settings, à la Twelve angry men: we expect the movie to proceed as such, and I must say I was feeling content about it. Then the scope expands : we leave the house and explore some parts of the cities culminating in "active" scenes, where the movie started very cerebrally. The way we discover new places in the film gave me a feeling that is hard to describe: like each scene and shot was deliberate, a bit like in theatre.

The first part of the movie is very much a play: all characters go in and out of the same place, and deliberate about  the moral thing to do, in a Greek-like way. But then comes movement, and we are thrust into cinema, into multiple places. Where the kidnapping was hidden, where all violence appeared off screen, we step into a graphic world. The bar scene and the dope alley scene are two masterpieces. The shot with the city lights reflecting on the car during the night drive is one of the most beautiful I have seen.

High and low has for me one characteristic of the great "all encompassing" movies: it is curious about every part of society: the whole human dance. We have the factory director, the garbage man, the train driver, the medic, the chauffeur, the dealers, the addicts, the policemen, the journalists. Everybody has a little something to say, and is seen. The scene where the train conductor explains the difference between the trains and mimics their sounds is incredible, although it lasts twenty seconds or so, it's just very good.

Another thing the movie has is a little subtle humor amid the darkness. There is a joke in the movie: how the policemen following the killer look so much like cops that it is hard for them not to be seen. It becomes comical afterwards to see them disguised as sailors, as dope fiends, crouching one moment and then running as fast as they can to catch the killer. It's just one joke, with variations on it during the movie, but because it is subtle and appears amid real sadness and tragedy, it creates what is for me the "proper" tone of life: that is a touch of humor as tragedy happens, which lightens up everything, but also does not sugarcoat.

Ultimately what made me love the movie I wager, is its morality, or at least the way I understood it. To me, High and Low is very much a Dostoevskian piece. The way is see it, good is shown to ultimately triumph over evil in the last scene of the movie, when the killer is laughing maniacally to try to hide that he is afraid. This is almost word for word or "image for image" what the the dying priest says at the end of The Brothers Karamazov part one : "Though the sinner will laugh in the face of the christian, he has been irrevocably touched by the experience of love: it is now only a question of time before he repents: it is inevitable." When the killer "breaks" at the end of the movie, in front of the factory manager who just sits there, at most concerned by the fate of the lad, this is the proof of the triumph of good. The killer wanted to be hated by the factory manager, because he ruined his life: but the factory manager tells him: "why do you want us to hate each other? there is no reason for that". In that masterful moment, the factory manager goes beyond the crime and the circumstances of their meeting: he reaches to the very human that stands in front of him, naturally, not out of contrivance, and it is this effortless movement of love that conquers the bad faith of the killer.

That is why I love this movie, because its theme is essentially: the triumph of good over evil, and because it showcases an inspiring man, in the person of Mr Gondo. As the policeman says in the train "isn't he something?".

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Your English seems great, I never would have guessed it's not your first language. 

The thing that impresses me most about the film is the framing and blocking of the cinematography (all Kurosawa films really)
2. I was going through the films of Kurosawa a year or two back because I hadn't seen them but had of course always heard about his genius. It was a real education. Because his samurai films overshadow the rest of his accomplishments to the average film watcher, I had no idea he even made 'present day' films. So I was thrilled to watch Ikiru, Stray Dog, The Bad Sleep Well, and his more experimental films like Dodes'ka-den and Dersu Uzala. But it was High and Low that BLEW MY MIND. Blew my absolute doors off. It's probably my favorite Kurosawa now. I concur that it looks like a play off the top. For a while, I was starting to think that the entire movie was just going to take place in his apartment. But the morals, the tension, the grey areas, the pressure and questions about humanity and class were incredible to witness. Had me hooked from the get go and keep me enthralled until the end. An amazing piece of work.
3. *High and Low* isn't in my top five of Kurosawa's movies, but it's still so good.

There's a shot in this movie that really hit me. It's in the first segment, when they've just learned that it's the chauffeur's son who was abducted *after* Gondo has decided to pay everything they demand. Suddenly the situation has changed, and it's not at all clear what's going to happen, and the chauffeur throws himself on the ground at Gondo's feet with this look of such convincing anguish that it completely broke through that barrier of "I'm watching a performance."
4. I have an specific and contrarian opinion of this film (which I do enjoy as an amazing piece of cinema). When viewed without an understanding of the historical and political moment of 1960s Japan and its turmoil, the film reads as humanist and neutral. However, in its day, in the context of the politics of its moment, the film was reactionary and conservative (and was considered as such, at the time, by scholars and other filmmakers). Modern viewers don't get that the villain is intended as a mocking caricature of the ANPO student protesters. Government, corporations, police, and press working together in lock-step synchronization to supress the bad guys? Yaaay authoritarian systems of discipline and punishment.

Also -- and again this is just opinion -- but "the triumph of good over evil" is a pleasing narrative. But does that accurately describe what is actually occurring in the world right now, or what was happening in 1963?
  - I think this is mostly how I feel about the film as well. Except I don't think it reads as particularly humanist and neutral without that understanding. I really enjoyed just about every other aspect of the film, and think it showcases just how talented of a director Kurosawa was and why his films are so highly praised, but I was still left quite disappointed by it on first viewing given how often I had seen it praised as being a humanist and progressive examination and critique of post-war Japan. 


I do suspect a lot of people may see what you wrote and think that you are being too harsh as it is simply a product of its time. And to that I cannot agree. Having watch a lot of Japanese films from that era I often find that Kurosawa's work does stand out as being more conservative than many of his contemporaries.
    - Thank you. You 100% get what I am saying

## 2. (Marty Supreme Spoilers) Was Marty’s ultimate goal to beat Endo, or to become World Champion?

**Score:** 0
**Body:** I’m having a debate with a friend about this. He thinks Marty goes to Japan only to get revenge for losing the final to Endo and to prove to himself that he’s the best. I see it differently. I think he goes to the World Championship because he genuinely wants to win the title and become World Champion.

In my opinion, beating Endo in the exhibition match is more of a consolation and something to ease the sting of that past loss rather than the goal he’s been chasing throughout the entire film. It proves something to himself, sure, but I don’t think it was ever his ultimate objective. By the end, he also seems to realize there are things more important than just being the best.

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I see beating Endo and being world champion as exactly the same thing. When he eventually beats Endo, he says to him "good luck, I hope you win" (the championship). If Endo goes on to win it, Marty can call himself the best in the world. He seems to want this. Both you and your friend are arguing the same thing.
  - Endo had apparently won a single event and was not the world champion.  Marty saying “I hope you win” indicates that there is still some doubt whether Endo would even win the championship.  Marty beats him in a single spur-of-the-moment exhibition set. 

Marty wanted to be on Wheaties boxes and be the fax of international table tennis.  All of these takes that him winning this one set is some type of catharsis is giving Safdie’s poor writing way too much of a pass because it makes no sense. 
    - Yea I know, Endo was _going to_ the Championships. Like I said. Him saying "I hope you win" doesn't indicate anything about his chances. His chances are obviously pretty good if we're to believe Marty's were, or any of the other things we're told in the film. I didn't say it was catharsis (not the right word, either), I just tried to answer OP's question. You have a stick up your arse.
  - Let me ask you a hypothetical question. If Marty had been allowed to participate in the World Championship and ended up winning the finals, but never faced Endo in the tournament bracket (he would lost in semis to other player for example), would he still have achieved his goal if they never played against each other again? That’s what we’re mainly debating. We know that at the end of the movie he feels satisfied.
2. He seems profoundly disappointed to hear from the association head that he won’t be able to play in the championships. It’s only after that that he decides to challenge Endo to a “real” game. 

But I also don’t think of it as a consolation. He realizes that his goal is to be the greatest, and that he doesn’t need to go to the championships to prove it. He already considers himself to be the best in the world, except that he lost to Endo. He realizes that he can rematch Endo right there, but it requires angering his vampiric benefactor.
  - The Marty at any other point in the movie would have used his exhibition win to hustle his way into the bigger tournament. Or he would have coerced Rockwell to force him into the tournament if Marty agreed to stop beating Endo.  There’s also a million other tournaments he can play in plus the world championships the next year.  There is no realistic reason for Marty to “stop” pursuing his dreams (especially when he just said two days before that failure doesn’t even enter his consciousness).  
  - Let me ask you a hypothetical question. If Marty had been allowed to participate in the World Championship and ended up winning the finals, but never faced Endo in the tournament bracket (he would lost in semis to other player for example), would he still have achieved his goal if they never played against each other again? That’s what we’re mainly debating. We know that at the end of the movie he feels satisfied.
    - Yes, I think he would have been satisfied. I don’t recall him being obsessed with Endo specifically. Marty strikes me as someone who moves goal posts, in order to manipulate others or to keep his massive ego afloat.
      - I see it that way too.

## 3. Marty Supreme: A largely mediocre film that keeps meandering.

**Score:** 0
**Body:** So I just watched Marty Supreme and have some thoughts. Personally thought it was a mediocre 3/5 movie that isn't as good and profound as the hype suggests. It's a movie about the banality of ambition for ambition's sake based around a character who is more of a sniveling grifter than someone actually obsessed with the game. Marty's more obsessed with the idea of his own greatness than he is with ping pong. His dream ends up being a nothing burger at the end and he ultimately has to settle down for the very thing he resented: a mediocre, mundane life with responsibility towards family.

All very good but I really wish the movie tried to present this in a better way. I think it falters after he returns from the first ping pong trip and the dog plotline ensues, which I think served no purpose other than trying to show us that Odessa A'zion's character would do anything for Marty and to keep him with herself. I personally didn't think that was necessary and the conceit of her character was well portrayed with the makeup injury scene anyways.

And Gwyneth Paltrow served no other purpose than to maybe provide some meta commentary on the art of method acting while playing a washed up actress being a washed up actress herself (no disrespect to her). But really what is the point of Marty sleeping with her? To show us that he is a deadbeat grifting scoundrel with no sense of responsibility? I think that could have been shown in a better way in his interactions with Kevin O'Leary's character. You can also see that in his interactions with Tyler The Creator's character.

Also I interpreted the ending as a failure on Marty's part. I have myself been in similar situations where ambition is so extreme that settling for anything less doesn't feel right. I am not convinced that Marty Mauser will not abandon his child for another impulsive ping pong spree to Japan. His tears read more like despair at the loss of his dream than an acceptance of his life's new, very banal and mundane direction. And I also don't think Marty is a bad person. He is a hustler and is a product of his environment, background and upbringing. It doesn't feel good to settle for less. Some people try their hardest to achieve more than what they have been given in life and that doesn't make them a bad person. I sympathized a lot with Marty's character and constantly kept wishing that he was smart enough to achieve it. But he was not, a point the movie conveys very well. The entire point of his character: that he is not smart enough.  I just really wish Josh Safdie stayed true to the character.

Ultimately I think the film just meanders trying to make a spectacle more than doing anything substantial. The entire movie felt like a subplot. I really wish there was more nuance to Marty's character. He felt more like a caricature of his character's archetype than a real person. Anyways Timothée Chalamet is good as a sniveler. At his most charismatic in one of his least charismatic roles. And I think that's all I have to say.

4. I think this reads more like moral frustration than criticism. Describing Marty as a “snivelling grifter” or Paltrow as a “washed up actress” closes things down too quickly. Once the characters are reduced to types, there isn’t much room left for ambiguity, or for the possibility that the film’s roughness might be deliberate.

If Marty is more in love with the idea of greatness than with ping pong itself, that feels less like a flaw than the point. The film seems interested in ambition detached from craft — striving as performance. The chaos isn’t evidence that he’s listless; it’s part of the predicament he’s created. He lives inside instability because that’s the only space where the fantasy can survive.

The structure reflects that. The story lurches because he lurches. His drive is real, but it isn’t disciplined. That’s different from drift.

I also read the ending differently. He wins on his own terms, and in the scene with his nemesis — the “vampire” bit before the match — he laughs it off and performs. That suggests he isn’t only self-mythology. When it matters, he can deliver.

After that, choosing a normal life doesn’t look like defeat so much as proportion. He tests the dream and reaches its limit. That feels less like surrender and more like recognition.

The disagreement may just be about what counts as success. If you’re looking for transcendence, the film will seem thin. I’m more drawn to the bungled and the botched

5. I thought it was way more concerned with style over substance. Sometimes the era mixing was jarring and I didn’t buy that Timothee was from the 50s once. He seemed like a Safdie scumbag from todays era plucked and sent to the past. 

But I agree the style was enough to keep me interested 3.5/5.
  - It's tough to say whether he's an accurate period character, since most people here weren't adults at that time, and period movies tended to ignore these types of people who are antithetical to what the 50s symbolizes. I kind of liked that I was able to almost forget it took place in the 50s because the movie doesn't repeatedly beat you over the head with it. It feels more like real people than the almost mythological period archetypes you'd see in something like a Scorsese movie. 

Marty is also supposed to be out of his time. It's interesting to watch precisely *because* he feels out of place and is maneuvering a world that is not ready for his type of manipulation. I think if they'd given him a phoney old New York accent or something, it would have felt more contrived.

What's interesting about this one is that it is structurally not very different from *Good Time* or *Uncut Gems*, which were not nearly as polarizing. Both of those films present a morally compromised, obsessive character, and follow him through a series of "and then this happened" situations that sort of tangentially meander out of an established scenario. The focus is almost entirely lost through the second act, mostly presenting interesting character dynamics that take some active thought to place in terms of plot and justification. Then, the third act hastily brings things together and in the last couple of minutes signs off a thesis that may feel rushed, but also somehow works even if the preceding events didn't directly feed into it.  
  
"What purpose did this character serve?" It adds richness and authenticity, imo. It's not like Jaws, where the structure is perfect and every character plays a role with the exact number of lines needed. These feel more like real people coming in and out of someone's life during the moments we get to see. It's messier, and that's the charm that people love about the Safdies' work. It is, of course, a gamble, because as we can see, it did not work for a lot of people in *Marty Supreme*. I think that mostly comes down to the revisiting of the house for the dog, which was unmistakably redundant beyond what audiences will accept. For many this was the fatal flaw, or at least drew attention to the fact that we had been wandering for over an hour.

Another issue here was expectation. People expect a bit of a mess from a crime drama/thriller, with out of control circumstances. But when the framework of a film is a sports biopic, there's a lot of expectation that's hard to shake. Everyone knows he's going to face Koto again. But when it feels like nothing that is happening is correlated to that upcoming event, you just end up sort of waiting for something that will actually get Marty closer. We do learn a lot more about Marty, but we get very little in terms of plot.

  
Anyways, I really liked it, but I understand how a lot of it wouldn't work. I just think it's an exceptional case study in critical reception changing significantly from one movie to another when they are both adaptations of the same template.

6. I really enjoyed it, but unlike you I didn't go in with preconceived notions based on the hype. No film EVER lives up to the hype after all. 

It seems you've decided that Marty is supposed to be a certain character, and that the film has a certain message, but then you criticise the film for not doing what you expect. 

You also seem to be assuming that the film is failing for not taking a moral standpoint on Marty's actions. However it doesn't claim to. We can empathise with a character who isn't moral, and be interested in what he does, without having to endorse his actions. I would say the issue of whether Marty is a bad person is neither here nor there. He's a struggling young man with lofty goals, and that's all that matters.

My suggestion for critiquing a film would be to start with what's actually there, and to assume it is what the director intended. Then to go from there to how it makes you feel, rather than complaining because the film isn't something else.

## 4. Arrival isn't about Louise choosing to accept her fate — it's about the impossibility of choice itself

**Score:** 0
**Body:** I rewatched *Arrival* last night for the first time in a few years, and something about the ending hit me completely differently. I've been thinking about determinism since I was 19 — one of those late-night thoughts that started as "huh, interesting" and slowly grew into something I can't stop turning over in my head. Recently I finished *War and Peace*, read Sapolsky's *Determined*, and now *Arrival* again, and they all seem to be circling the same idea from different angles.

Most people walk away from *Arrival* seeing Louise's decision at the end as this beautiful, tragic choice. She knows her daughter will die young. She knows the marriage will fall apart. She chooses to live that life anyway because the joy is worth the pain. It's framed as almost heroic — accepting a future of grief because love matters more.

But here's what I think the film is actually doing, and why it's so much more philosophically radical than that reading suggests: Louise doesn't *choose* anything. The film's own internal logic makes choice impossible.

Think about how the heptapod language works. Louise doesn't just learn to predict the future — she starts to *experience* time the way the heptapods do. Their writing is circular, with no beginning or end. You can't read a heptapod sentence from left to right because the entire symbol is written simultaneously. The end affects the beginning just as much as the beginning affects the end. When Louise fully internalizes this language, she stops experiencing time as a linear sequence of moments and starts perceiving her entire timeline at once.

The "flashbacks" we see throughout the film aren't flashbacks at all. They're flash-forwards. Louise isn't remembering her daughter — she's *perceiving* her daughter, across all of time, in the same moment. Past, present, and future collapse into a single experience.

So when we get to that final scene — Louise with Ian, knowing what's coming, supposedly "choosing" to say yes — what does choice even mean in that context? If she's experiencing the entire timeline simultaneously, then the decision to have Hannah is already part of the pattern. It's not that she *makes* a choice and then lives with it. The choice, the daughter, the loss, the grief — they're all already there, written into the structure of her life like one of those circular heptapod symbols.

This is where Ted Chiang's original story "Story of Your Life" makes things even clearer. Chiang brings in the variational principle from physics — the idea that there are two completely different ways to describe how reality works. The causal way is what we're used to: event A causes event B causes event C, moving forward through time. But the variational way describes a system where the entire path is determined by both the starting point *and* the endpoint simultaneously. The system doesn't move step by step — it already "knows" where it will end up.

Heptapods live in the second framework. They don't experience cause and effect. They experience the entire shape of events all at once. And by the end of the film, so does Louise.

I kept thinking about the bookshelf scene in *Interstellar* while watching this. Cooper ends up in that four-dimensional space where he can see all of time laid out simultaneously, just like Louise. But Nolan gives Cooper a way out — he lets him intervene, send messages, change things. He gets to play the hero who saves the world through sheer force of will.

Villeneuve doesn't give Louise that escape. She sees everything, and she can't change any of it. Because if you're experiencing all of time at once, there's no "before" the decision where you could have chosen differently. The decision is just another point in the pattern.

When I was 19, I started tracing causal chains backward. Every event has its own set of causes. Those causes have their own causes. You can keep going back — through your childhood, your parents' lives, their parents, evolutionary history, the formation of the planet, the beginning of the universe. At every single moment, all the prerequisites come together and the outcome can only be one. That thought started as something kind of interesting to consider and over eight years it's grown into something I genuinely can't resolve.

*Arrival* is the only film I've seen that takes this idea seriously and follows it all the way to its logical conclusion. If the universe works this way — if everything is already written — then Louise's "choice" at the end isn't a choice at all. It's an acceptance. Not acceptance in the sense of "I could do otherwise but I won't," but acceptance in the sense of seeing the shape of your life and recognizing that this is what it is, what it always was, what it could only ever be.

There's a character in *War and Peace* — Kutuzov, the old general — who understands this in a way none of the other characters do. Everyone around him is trying to control events, make brilliant strategic decisions, shape history through force of will. Kutuzov just... accepts that he doesn't control anything. He's not passive, exactly. He acts. But he knows he's not the author of what happens. Tolstoy presents him as the wisest character in the entire thousand-page book.

I think Louise ends up in the same place. Not because she's given up or because she's being heroically self-sacrificing, but because she's seen the whole shape and understands that there was never a version of this where she chose differently.

There's this experiment from the 1980s — the Libet experiment — where researchers measured brain activity while people made simple decisions. They found that the brain activity associated with the decision started about half a second before the person became consciously aware of deciding. Your brain "decides" before "you" do. It's a tiny gap, just 500 milliseconds, but it points to something much bigger: the possibility that what we experience as free will is actually just consciousness catching up to processes that were already in motion.

*Arrival* takes that idea and expands it across an entire lifetime. Louise's consciousness catches up to the whole thing at once.

So here's what I keep turning over: does this make the film more moving, or less? If Louise genuinely had a choice — if she could have walked away, lived a different life, avoided the pain — then her decision to embrace it anyway is tragic and beautiful. But if there was never any choice, if the entire timeline is fixed and she's just becoming aware of what already is... does that change how we're supposed to feel about the ending?

I think it might actually make it more profound. Because it's not about heroism anymore. It's not about being brave enough to accept suffering. It's about something harder to articulate — the experience of seeing your life as a complete shape, outside of time, and recognizing yourself in it. Not choosing it. Just... seeing it.

What do you think? Does the deterministic reading make Louise's story more meaningful or does it undercut the emotional weight? And if the film really is suggesting that choice is an illusion, what does that mean for how we watch it — how we relate to her experience?
